title: Brocklesby Lancaster Memorial
crew_summary: 7 killed including pilot Plt Off. John Joseph F. Ryan
narrative: The night of 3/4 March 1945 was one of the most extraordinary and little-known episodes of the air war. F/O Jack Ryan, a 33-year-old Australian, took off from RAF Spilsby at 18:42 to join 212 Lancasters attacking the Ladbergen aqueducts on the Dortmund-Ems Canal. The raid was successful -- over 100 metres of canal bank destroyed, both aqueducts in ruins. But as the bombers turned for home, something unprecedented happened. The Luftwaffe launched Operation Gisela: approximately 200 German night fighters followed the bomber stream back across the North Sea and prowled the skies over eastern England, attacking bombers as they approached their home airfields with navigation lights on and undercarriages down. It was a reversal of every established tactic. At 01:00 hours, with Lincolnshire spread out below and safety minutes away, a Ju88C found Lancaster ME442. The German pilot attacked, and the Lancaster crashed into Grannington Park on the Brocklesby Estate, among ancient trees. All seven crew were killed. The youngest were 19-year-old flight engineer Thomas Jarman and 19-year-old rear gunner William Rogan. Ryan, at 33, was one of the older pilots still flying operations. The Earl of Yarborough, owner of the Brocklesby Estate, was so moved that he commissioned a copper and bronze plaque giving the names of all seven crew, and had it attached to a large tree that had been snapped off by the explosion of the crashing Lancaster.

crew.name: Thomas Handley Jarman
crew.burial_location: Stretford Cemetery, Lancashire, Sec W, Grave 40

crew.name: Richard Raven Russell
crew.burial_location: Hove New Cemetery, Sussex, Block 3, Grave 125

cause_of_loss: Shot down by Ju88C night fighter during Operation Gisela (Luftwaffe intruder operation over England)
operation_target: Returning from bombing Ladbergen aqueducts (Dortmund-Ems Canal)
memorial_details: The memorial is a copper and bronze plaque mounted on a Wellingtonia tree trunk in Grannington Park on the Brocklesby Estate, near Great Limber. Commissioned by the Earl of Yarborough. The site is on private estate land but accessible by arrangement. A 75th anniversary commemoration service was held in March 2020 in the woodland. Condition: maintained by the estate.

external_narratives.summary: Comprehensive account of the loss of Lancaster ME442 KM-V on the night of 3/4 March 1945 during Operation Gisela. F/O Jack Ryan RAAF and his crew were shot down by a Ju88C night fighter as they returned from bombing the Ladbergen aqueducts on the Dortmund-Ems Canal.
key_details: Part of Operation Gisela - approximately 200 German night fighters attacked returning bombers over England

external_narratives.summary: The IBCC documented the 75th anniversary commemoration event held in the woodland of the Brocklesby Estate in March 2020. The event honoured the seven crew of Lancaster ME442 shot down during Operation Gisela.
key_details: Event held in Grannington Park woodland on Brocklesby Estate

external_narratives.summary: The 44 Squadron Association newsletter describes the Brocklesby Park Memorial, noting the copper and bronze plaque commissioned by the Earl of Yarborough was attached to a tree snapped off by the Lancaster's explosion. The site is treated as consecrated ground.
key_details: Plaque commissioned by Earl of Yarborough
Located in Grannington Park on Brocklesby Estate

external_narratives.summary: IWM records the memorial to the crew of Lancaster ME442 at the Brocklesby Estate, Lincolnshire.
